mnemonics.title: Decentralization Dimensions
mnemonics.content: Remember ALP: Architectural (location of nodes), Logical (single/many systems), Political (who controls). Think 'A' for Actual hardware, 'L' for Logic in code, 'P' for Power/control.

mnemonics.title: Merkle Tree Benefits
mnemonics.content: PARE: Proof of inclusion without downloading full data, Audit trail verification, Root hash summarizes entire data, Efficient O(log n) proof size.

mnemonics.title: Blockchain Trilemma
mnemonics.content: DSS: Decentralization vs Security vs Scalability - think 'Don't Sacrifice Security' - security is non-negotiable in most designs.

mnemonics.title: DAO Characteristics
mnemonics.content: TOPS: Token-based voting, Open source code, Programmatic execution, Self-governing - no central authority.

acronyms.usage: Application running on P2P network with blockchain backend and open-source code.
Organization governed by smart contracts and token holder voting, without hierarchical management.
Decentralized storage system enabling key-value lookup across peer-to-peer networks.
Cryptographic algorithm using elliptic curves for digital signatures in blockchain.
Method to verify transactions using Merkle proofs without downloading full blockchain.
Ethereum's state data structure combining Merkle tree with Patricia trie for efficient storage.

visualTips.visualization: Picture an upside-down tree: leaves (transactions) at bottom are hashed in pairs, merging upward until single Merkle Root at top. Like family tree going up - each parent represents combined hash of two children.
Imagine interconnected blocks as chain links - each block contains hash of previous block. To alter one block, you'd need to break all subsequent links, computationally infeasible.
Think of a slider: left end = fully centralized (one server, one authority), right end = fully decentralized (everyone equal, no central point). Real systems sit somewhere on this spectrum.
Circular flow: Token Holders → Proposal → Discussion → Voting → Smart Contract Execution → Results Recorded on Blockchain → Back to Token Holders for oversight.

analogies.analogy: Traditional database is like a company's private filing cabinet - controlled by IT department. Blockchain is like a shared Google Doc where everyone has a copy, changes require group agreement, and history is visible to all.
Like proving a document exists in a massive library by showing a chain of library index cards - you don't need the whole library, just the path proving your document's location.
Centralized is like putting all eggs in one basket - if basket breaks, all eggs lost. Decentralized is like spreading eggs across multiple baskets - need to break multiple baskets to lose all eggs.
Like a vending machine: insert money (trigger), select item (condition), machine automatically dispenses (execution) - no human cashier needed, rules are programmed in.
